Geospatial Foundation Models — Live Review

GitHub
How to read this dashboard

This site is a self-updating literature review: every day it reads new papers that evaluate geospatial foundation models (AlphaEarth, TESSERA, Prithvi, Clay, …) and extracts their quantitative results. Every number shown here links back to a paper and a verbatim quote from it — nothing is editorialised.

Direction ▲ better ▼ worse ≈ parity
Colours always compare a foundation model against a task-specific model — a bespoke supervised model built for that exact task, i.e. what a practitioner would otherwise use — on one dataset. Better = the foundation model wins, worse = the task-specific model wins, parity = effectively equal. Model-vs-model results (e.g. TESSERA vs AlphaEarth) are shown in the details but never drive the colours; a cell with only those is left neutral.
Evidence labels
consensus ≥3 papers and ≥80% agree on the direction · contested ≥3 papers but they disagree · thin only 1–2 papers so far · gap nobody has looked yet.
Label regime
How much training data the downstream task used: label-rich (≥75% of labels), label-partial, label-scarce (≤10%). The By benchmark and grouped views keep regimes separate. But most papers never report it — see the evidence panel below; label efficiency is the central promise of foundation models and it is the thing this corpus is least able to check.
Self-evaluation
The paper's authors helped create the very foundation model they evaluate (checked mechanically via author overlap with the model's original paper). Such papers tend to report wins — the toggle above excludes them.
How the headline verdict is counted
Numbers are never averaged across datasets or metrics — an mIoU and an F1 are never added up. Instead each paper gets one vote (the majority direction of its own results), and the votes are counted. That vote does pool papers that used different datasets, metrics and label regimes, which is a real limitation: pooling can produce agreement that no single benchmark supports. So every verdict also shows its strongest single benchmark — the same data, same protocol check — and says so plainly when the two disagree.
Same-dataset evidence
For the strict apples-to-apples cut, use the By benchmark tab: it groups results by the exact dataset, so a disagreement there is a real disagreement rather than an artefact of different data.
Diagnostic tasks
Linear probing of frozen embeddings interrogates a representation; it is not the alternative a practitioner would otherwise build. It is listed separately and never counted as evidence that foundation models beat the task-specific option.

Each row is one claim from one paper. The quote underneath is copied verbatim from the paper and was mechanically verified to appear in its text.

Every number on this site is traceable in ≤2 clicks to a paper and a verbatim supporting quote. Updated daily and fully automatic; the underlying data is one JSON file. Inference is subscription-only via a local CLI; this site holds no credentials.
